story:
I’m thirteen days into playing princess-in-a-tower and the hills beyond my tiny stone window are still empty of any knight on any steed. When I signed up at the Knights Hall—dusty skirt, bright smile, and zero shame—they sold me The Path to Knighthood: a training program where real, breathing “princesses” get rescued so trainees don’t freeze up when actual royalty needs saving. Better PR than letting real princesses vanish or get eaten by their assigned dragons. The fine print was a mountain, and somewhere in there was a cheerful little note about death waivers. I skimmed it, dazzled by the gold and the chance to stop watching my mother limp to her corner seamstress job every dawn.

They told me two or three days. That’s the average rescue time. The pay requires “certain traits”—the kind that once earned me stares and comments—plus compliance with the big rule: never leave the tower. Do so, and the whole purse turns to smoke.

Now the water jug is frighteningly light, food dwindling to crumbs, and the bed feels like a straw-stuffed joke. They left needlework for “entertainment.” Day two, I glared at it on principle. Day five, I surrendered, stabbed my fingers bloody, and flung the yarn across the room with words my mother would pretend not to know. So much for idle, simpering princesses—I might owe them an apology.

Every hour drips by. I pace. I curse myself for being angry at a trainee for…being a trainee. I also curse the version of me who sprinted to volunteer because money sounded like miracle and heroics like a shortcut. I even glance at the window and decide firmly that hurling myself out of it for a few coins is the worst idea I’ve ever had. I stay because staying keeps the money real.

Then, across the hill I’ve studied every morning, a figure appears—small, limping, horse-less. My heart leaps. I shout, wave, scold myself for shouting from a tower, then don’t care. He’s slow; the limp buys me time to fight my wild red curls into something less feral. They provided a mirror so we don’t greet salvation looking like gutter ghosts. It doesn’t help.

Boots thud. My door swings wide. Harsh breathing fills the threshold—but no one steps in. “You can come in,” I offer, trying to sound regal. Silence. Maybe he needs permission. Maybe I’m doing princess wrong. “Sir Knight?” Still nothing. Irritation eclipses relief. I cross the room, edge into the shadowed doorway, and find…no one. Panic flares. I run back to the window, lean out, scan the ground. Empty. “No, no, no—he was here.” My hand slips. Air tears past my face. The ground surges up. I scream—

—and sit bolt upright on the straw mattress. The needlework slumps in the corner where I threw it. Pots and pans littered the countertops. The window still frames indifferent hills. It was a dream. I am still in the tower. The rule still holds. The money still depends on my patience. Somewhere, a limping, horse-less trainee is learning his way across a map; I watch him pinch the curling edge flat and run his finger along a crooked ink path that will bring him here. I breathe, swallow my panic, and wait for the day he crests the hill for real.
